title: AI And Code Ownership: Who Is Responsible For Generated Code?

content_html:
Imagine your AI assistant just produced 200 lines of code. Legally, you may not own a single line of it but legally, you're still on the hook for every bug it ships. Here's the gap between those two sentences, and what it means for the code you merge tomorrow.

That's the gap this whole topic lives in. The tool that produced the code can't be sued. The model can't be held accountable. The vendor's contract has already made it clear, in language most developers never read, that responsibility flows downhill, to the human who pressed Tab.

The interesting part isn't that this gap exists. It's that almost nobody in engineering treats it like it does. We've spent two years celebrating the productivity, and a much smaller fraction of that time figuring out what to do when the velocity meets a copyright claim, a compliance audit, or a production incident. Let's break it down: what courts have actually said, what your tool's contract actually says, where the legal cliffs are, and how a sane engineering team handles the whole thing without either banning AI or pretending the question doesn't exist.

The Handoff That Nobody Signed

Software ownership used to be a settled question. You wrote the code, your employer paid you to write it, your employment contract assigned the copyright to them, the company shipped it under whatever license they preferred. Three parties (you, your employer, the user) and the lines between them were clean. If something broke, the user complained to the company, the company looked at the commit history, and someone got a quiet conversation in a one-on-one.

AI assistants slide a fourth party into that picture, and the contract for that party doesn't look like any of the others. The model didn't sign your employment agreement. The vendor isn't part of your release process. Their EULA isn't shaped like a hiring contract; it's shaped like a disclaimer. You get the suggestion, you keep the suggestion, and you also keep everything that comes attached to it: the bugs, the license obligations, the security holes, and whatever future regulator decides to inspect how the suggestion got there.

That handoff happens silently. There's no checkbox in your IDE that says "I accept legal responsibility for this completion." You just hit Tab. The interface is designed to feel like autocomplete, and most developers extend the mental model of autocomplete to it: the editor is helping me type faster, the code is still mine, my ownership of the codebase is unchanged. That mental model is almost right and almost completely wrong, depending on which question you're asking.

What Courts Have Actually Decided

Two things sit at the heart of the legal picture in the United States right now, and both of them surprise developers when they hear them.

The first: in the U.S., copyright protection requires a human author. That isn't a new rule. It's the same rule that decided a monkey couldn't claim copyright on a selfie back in 2018, but it got freshly stress-tested in Thaler v. Perlmutter, a case about an image generated by an AI system without meaningful human input. In March 2025, the D.C. Circuit affirmed the lower court's ruling: the Copyright Act of 1976 "requires all eligible work to be authored in the first instance by a human being." No human author, no copyright. The output sits in the public domain.

The second, which is where developers actually live: the U.S. Copyright Office spent 2024 and early 2025 trying to draw a line for AI-assisted work that does involve a human. In January 2025, the Office published guidance saying that AI outputs are eligible for copyright only when a human contributes "sufficient expressive elements." Prompting alone, even sophisticated, iterative prompting, isn't enough. One comment quoted in the Office's report reached for a metaphor that hit developers harder than they expected: repetitive prompting is like spinning a roulette wheel. The human chose, but the human did not control the expressive elements of the output with enough specificity to be considered the author.

That metaphor matters more than the wording around it. "Tab to accept" is, in copyright terms, spinning a wheel. "Tab to accept, then rewrite four lines, restructure the function, add a guard clause, and integrate it into a class you already designed" is something else. That's where the human contribution starts becoming the kind of expressive control the Office is looking for.

The practical fallout: code that comes out of your AI tool more or less untouched may not be your company's property the way the code your senior engineer wrote last week is. It isn't stolen, but it also isn't copyrightable to your employer in any way that gives them the usual enforcement options. They can't sue someone who copies it. They can't claim it as a defendable asset. They can publish it, ship it, modify it, but the legal moat around it is thinner than they think.

The picture changes once you cross a border. The United Kingdom has had Section 9(3) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 on the books since the late 1980s, and it explicitly addresses "computer-generated works," assigning authorship to "the person by whom the arrangements necessary for the creation of the work are undertaken." India follows a similar approach. The EU is in the middle of a more ambitious reframing through the AI Act, which we'll come back to. The point: there is no single global answer right now. The code your team writes from a London office may have a clearer ownership story than the same code written from San Francisco, on the same model, in the same week.

What Your Tool's Contract Actually Says

Here is the sentence in GitHub's Copilot Product Specific Terms that does most of the work, and the sentence nobody quotes at AI adoption meetings:

"You retain all responsibility for your Code, including Suggestions you include in your Code or reference to develop your Code."

That sentence is doing two things at once. It hands you something: GitHub says it doesn't claim ownership of the suggestions, so they don't show up as a competing claim against your codebase. And it hands you something else: every legal risk that travels with those suggestions. Open-source license obligations from training data. Copyright claims from other developers whose code looked similar. Defects in the suggestion itself. The vendor's terms are explicit that you are responsible for determining whether your use of an output requires a third-party license and for complying with any such license.

If you read GitHub's Customer Agreement the way developers read README files, skimming for the install instructions, you miss that line. If you read it the way a lawyer reads it, that line is the whole document.

This shape isn't unique to Copilot. The same pattern shows up in nearly every AI coding assistant's terms: vendor disclaims ownership of outputs, customer accepts responsibility for outputs. The reason it's the same pattern is that it's the only shape that lets the vendor operate. Imagine the alternative, where the vendor warrants that every suggestion is clean of license obligations, free of bugs, and immune to copyright challenge. They can't make that promise about a model trained on the entire public corpus of GitHub. So the contract pushes the risk to the only party who can plausibly evaluate it: the human merging the code.

The model can't read your codebase's license terms. The vendor doesn't know what regulator your industry answers to. The IDE doesn't know whether the project you're working on is a hobby site or a payments processor. The only place to anchor responsibility is where the merge button lives.

The Indemnification Gap Nobody Mentions In Onboarding

Here's where the contract gets uncomfortable, and where most engineering org charts have a blind spot.

GitHub does offer IP indemnification for suggestions, if you're on a Business or Enterprise plan. If you get sued for copyright infringement because Copilot reproduced a snippet of someone's code, Microsoft will step in to defend you and pay damages under its Customer Copyright Commitment. The same pattern holds across the industry: most major AI coding tools offer some form of indemnification at the enterprise tier.

If you're on the Individual plan, none of that applies. You get the same model, the same suggestions, the same risk surface, and zero legal cover. If a court eventually rules that a snippet Copilot suggested to you was a substantial reproduction of someone else's GPL-licensed code, you, personally, are the one with the problem.

The reason this matters beyond the lawyer's office: a lot of professional software still ships from teams where engineers use their personal Copilot subscriptions, either because the company hasn't standardized yet, or because someone signed up six months before the company adopted an enterprise tier, or because they like their personal account's settings better. The code those engineers produce flows into the company codebase, and the indemnification that the company assumes it has, because the company is paying for Enterprise, doesn't actually cover suggestions that were made through the personal subscription.

Engineering leaders almost never audit this. The procurement team thinks the Enterprise contract covers everything. The developers don't know the contract draws a line at which subscription emitted the suggestion. The CISO finds out only if a lawsuit lands.

A clean accountability story requires every IDE, every developer machine, and every shared environment to be running the indemnified tier. "We bought Enterprise" is not the same as "every suggestion that touches our repo came from Enterprise."

The 65-Lexeme Filter And What It Doesn't Catch

If you read GitHub's safety documentation, you'll find a feature called the duplicate detection filter. The filter checks suggestions against public code on GitHub, and if a suggestion contains a code segment of about 65 lexemes or more (roughly 150 characters, the length of one or two paragraphs of dense code) and matches public code closely enough, the suggestion is suppressed. Administrators can enable this filter at the enterprise level, and most reasonable adoption guidelines tell you to keep it on.

The filter is the single most-cited mitigation when teams talk about Copilot license risk. It's also a great example of the gap between "a feature exists" and "the feature solves the problem."

The 65-lexeme threshold isn't arbitrary. It's a tradeoff between recall and usefulness. A filter that suppressed any three-token match against public code would suppress almost every Copilot suggestion, because three-token sequences appear in nearly all open-source projects. So the threshold sits well above that, in a band where matches are likely to be intentionally similar rather than accidentally similar. Reasonable design. The cost is that anything under that threshold flows through unfiltered.

A 30-lexeme function body that exactly matches a snippet from a GPL-licensed repository can land in your codebase, and the filter never sees it. A 50-lexeme idiom, a common algorithm implementation, a parser fragment, a serialization helper, can land identical to dozens of repositories without ever crossing the line. The filter isn't claiming to catch everything. It's claiming to catch the long-form near-duplicates that are most likely to support a copyright claim. The short-form stuff lives in a grey zone where the law itself hasn't decided what "substantially similar" means.

This is the part where you make a defensible call instead of a perfect one. The filter helps. Treating it as a complete solution doesn't. Treat it like the spam filter in your inbox: useful, often quietly correct, but not a substitute for not opening attachments from strangers.

The Regulators Don't Care Who Wrote It

The U.S. copyright conversation focuses on ownership. The European regulatory conversation focuses on responsibility for outcomes, and the framing is genuinely different.

Three pieces of EU legislation are converging into what some practitioners are calling the "Compliance Trinity": the AI Act, the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA), and the Product Liability Directive (PLD). Each one approaches AI from a different angle. The AI Act regulates AI systems by risk class, the CRA imposes cybersecurity obligations on connected products, the PLD attaches liability for defective products including software. They all share a common assumption: regulators do not care whether your code was written by a human, an AI tool, a contractor, or a small team of trained ferrets. They care about the product that reaches the market.

August 2, 2026 is the milestone most developers will hear about. It's when many of the AI Act's substantive obligations kick in, including transparency requirements for AI-generated content and active enforcement powers. Plain developer-assistance AI use generally doesn't drag a system into the high-risk category by itself, but AI used for things like worker evaluation or critical-component generation absolutely can. The classification depends on what the system does in the world, not on what tool helped write it.

The harder shift is the cultural one this implies. Before AI assistants, the was-it-written-carefully question and the is-the-product-safe question pointed at roughly the same thing: the people who wrote the code were also the people responsible for it. With AI assistants involved, the two questions can drift apart. The product can be unsafe even if everyone who reviewed the diff would swear they read it carefully. The regulator doesn't accept "the AI suggested it" as an explanation any more than they would accept "the contractor wrote it." You shipped it; you're responsible for it.

That's a clean framing, once you accept it. The discomfort comes from a half-formed expectation that AI tools come with a kind of distributed responsibility, that the vendor shares the blame because they trained the model. They don't. They explicitly don't, contractually. And the regulators have politely declined to invent that kind of shared responsibility either.

The Cultural Problem Nobody Warns You About

Outside the legal text, there's a softer pattern that quietly erodes accountability inside engineering teams, and it has nothing to do with contracts.

Researchers studying AI-assisted development have started to document something most senior engineers already feel: code that comes out of an AI assistant tends to get less careful review than code a human typed. Reviewers spend less time on it. They flag fewer issues. They're more likely to wave through a function that compiles and looks reasonable. Part of this is fatigue, because there's more code to review now than ever, now that the typing constraint that used to throttle output is gone. Part of it is implicit trust: the model has read more code than any reviewer alive, so its output gets a small unconscious authority bump. Part of it is just the design of the interface, which makes acceptance the default and rejection the friction.

The problem isn't that any of those reactions is unreasonable on its own. It's that they compound in the direction of less accountability, not more. The diff is twice as long, the review is half as careful, and the person who would normally have asked a sharp question about a defensive null check is busy scrolling through the next three suggestions. The unit of human attention per line of merged code drops, and so does the chance of catching the bug, the license issue, or the subtle authorization mistake that the model didn't realize it was making.

This isn't an argument against AI tools. It's the missing line on the dashboard that most teams haven't drawn yet: if we're producing more code, are we proportionally reviewing more carefully, or are we relaxing review because the volume forced us to? Nobody asks that question out loud. It would slow things down. And nothing about the regulatory framework above will save you from the answer.

What A Working Accountability Model Looks Like

You can't solve any of this with a Slack post. You can't solve it with a policy document either, unless the policy actually changes how code lands in your repo. The teams that handle this well do four mundane things, none of them clever.

They make tier ownership boring. Every developer, every shared environment, every CI runner that uses an AI assistant is on the indemnified tier. There is no personal-subscription escape hatch for company work. This is the procurement-level boring decision that closes most of the gap in one motion.

They make duplicate filtering on by default and verifiable. The filter is enabled in the admin console, and it's part of the periodic security review the same way SSO enforcement is. It's not a setting someone might have flipped on a Tuesday; it's an audited control.

They treat AI-generated code in code review the same as third-party dependencies. New code from a model is treated, mentally, the way a senior engineer would treat a copy-paste from a Stack Overflow answer in 2014. You ask: do I understand what this does, do I know the license shape it came from, would I sign my name to this if the model wasn't here to point at? That question is the entire ethical and legal apparatus, compressed into a sentence the reviewer can actually internalize.

They name an owner. Every PR has a human who is responsible for the code in it. Not the model. Not the vendor. Not "the team." A person, with a name, who will pick up the phone if the code breaks. If your tooling makes it hard to remember who that person is for AI-heavy PRs, that's a process problem worth fixing, because every other framework above only works if there's a name at the end of it.

Note
None of this requires a legal team or a compliance officer. The first three steps are admin-console settings and a review-checklist sentence. The fourth is a cultural norm that good teams already have for human-written code. The only new thing AI introduces is that you can't skip any of these because the volume increased.

The Sentence That Holds The Whole Thing

If you remember one thing from this post, make it this: every AI suggestion is a contribution from an anonymous third party who has read every open-source project on the internet, who you have no way to interview, and who your contract has explicitly placed outside the chain of responsibility.

That isn't a reason to stop using AI assistants. It's not even a reason to slow down. It's the actual shape of the relationship, and the friction in software engineering keeps coming back to it because that shape isn't going to dissolve on its own. Courts are converging on it. Vendors have already built their contracts around it. Regulators are about to enforce it.

The good news, if you can call it that, is that the answer was never in the model. It's in the part of the workflow that was already supposed to be the most boring and the most rigorous, the part where a human looks at a diff and decides whether to merge. That part has never been more important than it is now, and the cheapest thing your team can do to handle the whole ownership question is to make sure that part still happens, with the same care it had before the suggestions started arriving twice a minute.

You wrote the merge commit. You own it.

P.S. Thanks for taking the time to read this article! The ideas and opinions expressed here are my own. English is not my first language, so I use AI to help correct grammar and make my writing clearer and easier to read. If anything still sounds a little awkward, I appreciate your understanding!

Originally published at nazarboyko.com.

The fourth is a cultural norm that good teams already have for human-written code. The only new thing AI introduces is that you can't skip any of these because the volume increased. The Sentence That Holds The Whole Thing If you remember one thing from this post, make it this: every AI suggestion is a contribution from an anonymous third party who has read every open-source project on the internet, who you have no way to interview, and who your contract has explicitly placed outside the chain of responsibility. That isn't a reason to stop using AI assistants. It's not even a reason to slow down. It's the actual shape of the relationship, and the friction in software engineering keeps coming back to it because that shape isn't going to dissolve on its own. Courts are converging on it. Vendors have already built their contracts around it. Regulators are about to enforce it. The good news, if you can call it that, is that the answer was never in the model. It's in the part of the workflow that was already supposed to be the most boring and the most rigorous, the part where a human looks at a diff and decides whether to merge. That part has never been more important than it is now, and the cheapest thing your team can do to handle the whole ownership question is to make sure that part still happens, with the same care it had before the suggestions started arriving twice a minute. You wrote the merge commit. You own it. P.S. Thanks for taking the time to read this article! The ideas and opinions expressed here are my own. English is not my first language, so I use AI to help correct grammar and make my writing clearer and easier to read. If anything still sounds a little awkward, I appreciate your understanding! Originally published at nazarboyko.com.

blocks.html_en: The interesting part isn't that this gap exists. It's that almost nobody in engineering treats it like it does. We've spent two years celebrating the productivity, and a much smaller fraction of that time figuring out what to do when the velocity meets a copyright claim, a compliance audit, or a production incident. Let's break it down: what courts have actually said, what your tool's contract actually says, where the legal cliffs are, and how a sane engineering team handles the whole thing without either banning AI or pretending the question doesn't exist.

blocks.html_en: The Handoff That Nobody Signed

sentences.en: The Handoff That Nobody Signed
sentences.zh: 无人签署的交接

blocks.html_en: Software ownership used to be a settled question. You wrote the code, your employer paid you to write it, your employment contract assigned the copyright to them, the company shipped it under whatever license they preferred. Three parties (you, your employer, the user) and the lines between them were clean. If something broke, the user complained to the company, the company looked at the commit history, and someone got a quiet conversation in a one-on-one.

sentences.en: Software ownership used to be a settled question.
sentences.zh: 软件所有权曾经是一个已解决的问题。

sentences.en: You wrote the code, your employer paid you to write it, your employment contract assigned the copyright to them, the company shipped it under whatever license they preferred.
sentences.zh: 你编写了代码，你的雇主付钱给你编写代码，你的雇佣合同将版权分配给他们，公司根据他们喜欢的任何许可证发布它。

sentences.en: Three parties (you, your employer, the user) and the lines between them were clean.
sentences.zh: 三方（您、您的雇主、用户）以及他们之间的界限是清晰的。

sentences.en: If something broke, the user complained to the company, the company looked at the commit history, and someone got a quiet conversation in a one-on-one.
sentences.zh: 如果出现问题，用户会向公司投诉，公司会查看提交历史记录，然后有人进行一对一的安静对话。

blocks.html_en: AI assistants slide a fourth party into that picture, and the contract for that party doesn't look like any of the others. The model didn't sign your employment agreement. The vendor isn't part of your release process. Their EULA isn't shaped like a hiring contract; it's shaped like a disclaimer. You get the suggestion, you keep the suggestion, and you also keep everything that comes attached to it: the bugs, the license obligations, the security holes, and whatever future regulator decides to inspect how the suggestion got there.

sentences.en: AI assistants slide a fourth party into that picture, and the contract for that party doesn't look like any of the others.
sentences.zh: 人工智能助手将第四方滑入该图片中，该方的合同看起来与其他方都不一样。

sentences.en: The model didn't sign your employment agreement.
sentences.zh: 该模特没有签署你的雇佣协议。

sentences.en: The vendor isn't part of your release process.
sentences.zh: 供应商不参与您的发布过程。

sentences.en: Their EULA isn't shaped like a hiring contract; it's shaped like a disclaimer.
sentences.zh: 他们的 EULA 并不像雇佣合同；而是像雇佣合同一样。它的形状像免责声明。

sentences.en: You get the suggestion, you keep the suggestion, and you also keep everything that comes attached to it: the bugs, the license obligations, the security holes, and whatever future regulator decides to inspect how the suggestion got there.
sentences.zh: 你得到建议，保留建议，还保留其附带的所有内容：错误、许可义务、安全漏洞，以及未来监管机构决定检查建议如何到达那里的任何内容。

blocks.html_en: That handoff happens silently. There's no checkbox in your IDE that says "I accept legal responsibility for this completion." You just hit Tab. The interface is designed to feel like autocomplete, and most developers extend the mental model of autocomplete to it: the editor is helping me type faster, the code is still mine, my ownership of the codebase is unchanged. That mental model is almost right and almost completely wrong, depending on which question you're asking.

sentences.en: That handoff happens silently.
sentences.zh: 这种交接悄然发生。

sentences.en: There's no checkbox in your IDE that says "I accept legal responsibility for this completion."
sentences.zh: 您的 IDE 中没有复选框显示“我对此完成承担法律责任”。

sentences.en: You just hit Tab.
sentences.zh: 您只需按 Tab 键即可。

sentences.en: The interface is designed to feel like autocomplete, and most developers extend the mental model of autocomplete to it: the editor is helping me type faster, the code is still mine, my ownership of the codebase is unchanged.
sentences.zh: 该界面的设计感觉就像自动完成，大多数开发人员将自动完成的心理模型扩展到它：编辑器正在帮助我更快地打字，代码仍然是我的，我对代码库的所有权没有改变。

sentences.en: That mental model is almost right and almost completely wrong, depending on which question you're asking.
sentences.zh: 这种心理模型几乎是正确的，也几乎是完全错误的，这取决于你问哪个问题。

blocks.html_en: What Courts Have Actually Decided

sentences.en: What Courts Have Actually Decided
sentences.zh: 法院实际判决了什么

blocks.html_en: Two things sit at the heart of the legal picture in the United States right now, and both of them surprise developers when they hear them.

sentences.en: Two things sit at the heart of the legal picture in the United States right now, and both of them surprise developers when they hear them.
sentences.zh: 目前美国法律形势的核心有两件事，当开发商听到这两件事时，他们都感到惊讶。

blocks.html_en: The first: in the U.S., copyright protection requires a human author. That isn't a new rule. It's the same rule that decided a monkey couldn't claim copyright on a selfie back in 2018, but it got freshly stress-tested in Thaler v. Perlmutter, a case about an image generated by an AI system without meaningful human input. In March 2025, the D.C. Circuit affirmed the lower court's ruling: the Copyright Act of 1976 "requires all eligible work to be authored in the first instance by a human being." No human author, no copyright. The output sits in the public domain.

sentences.en: The first: in the U.S., copyright protection requires a human author.
sentences.zh: 第一：在美国，版权保护需要人类作者。

sentences.en: That isn't a new rule.
sentences.zh: 这不是一个新规则。

sentences.en: It's the same rule that decided a monkey couldn't claim copyright on a selfie back in 2018, but it got freshly stress-tested in Thaler v.
sentences.zh: 早在 2018 年，这条规则就决定猴子不能对自拍照提出版权主张，但它在泰勒诉美国案中接受了最新的压力测试。

sentences.en: Perlmutter, a case about an image generated by an AI system without meaningful human input.
sentences.zh: Perlmutter，一个关于人工智能系统在没有有意义的人类输入的情况下生成图像的案例。

sentences.en: In March 2025, the D.C.
sentences.zh: 2025 年 3 月，华盛顿特区

sentences.en: Circuit affirmed the lower court's ruling: the Copyright Act of 1976 "requires all eligible work to be authored in the first instance by a human being."
sentences.zh: 巡回法院维持了下级法院的裁决：1976 年《版权法》“要求所有符合资格的作品首先必须由人类创作”。

sentences.en: No human author, no copyright.
sentences.zh: 没有人类作者，没有版权。

sentences.en: The output sits in the public domain.
sentences.zh: 输出位于公共领域。

blocks.html_en: The second, which is where developers actually live: the U.S. Copyright Office spent 2024 and early 2025 trying to draw a line for AI-assisted work that does involve a human. In January 2025, the Office published guidance saying that AI outputs are eligible for copyright only when a human contributes "sufficient expressive elements." Prompting alone, even sophisticated, iterative prompting, isn't enough. One comment quoted in the Office's report reached for a metaphor that hit developers harder than they expected: repetitive prompting is like spinning a roulette wheel. The human chose, but the human did not control the expressive elements of the output with enough specificity to be considered the author.

sentences.en: The second, which is where developers actually live: the U.S. Copyright Office spent 2024 and early 2025 trying to draw a line for AI-assisted work that does involve a human.
sentences.zh: 第二个是开发者实际居住的地方：美国版权局在 2024 年和 2025 年初试图为确实涉及人类的人工智能辅助工作划定界限。

sentences.en: In January 2025, the Office published guidance saying that AI outputs are eligible for copyright only when a human contributes "sufficient expressive elements."
sentences.zh: 2025 年 1 月，该办公室发布了指导意见，称只有当人类贡献“足够的表达元素”时，人工智能输出才有资格获得版权。

sentences.en: Prompting alone, even sophisticated, iterative prompting, isn't enough.
sentences.zh: 单独的提示，即使是复杂的、迭代的提示，也是不够的。

sentences.en: One comment quoted in the Office's report reached for a metaphor that hit developers harder than they expected: repetitive prompting is like spinning a roulette wheel.
sentences.zh: 该办公室报告中引用的一条评论提出了一个比开发人员预期更严重的比喻：重复提示就像旋转轮盘赌。

sentences.en: The human chose, but the human did not control the expressive elements of the output with enough specificity to be considered the author.
sentences.zh: 人类选择了，但人类没有足够明确地控制输出的表达元素，无法被视为作者。

blocks.html_en: That metaphor matters more than the wording around it. "Tab to accept" is, in copyright terms, spinning a wheel. "Tab to accept, then rewrite four lines, restructure the function, add a guard clause, and integrate it into a class you already designed" is something else. That's where the human contribution starts becoming the kind of expressive control the Office is looking for.

sentences.en: That metaphor matters more than the wording around it.
sentences.zh: 这个比喻比它周围的措辞更重要。

sentences.en: "Tab to accept" is, in copyright terms, spinning a wheel.
sentences.zh: 从版权的角度来说，“Tab 接受”就是旋转轮子。

sentences.en: "Tab to accept, then rewrite four lines, restructure the function, add a guard clause, and integrate it into a class you already designed" is something else.
sentences.zh: “Tab 接受，然后重写四行，重构函数，添加一个保护子句，并将其集成到您已经设计的类中”是另一回事。

sentences.en: That's where the human contribution starts becoming the kind of expressive control the Office is looking for.
sentences.zh: 这就是人类贡献开始成为办公室所寻求的表达控制的地方。

blocks.html_en: The practical fallout: code that comes out of your AI tool more or less untouched may not be your company's property the way the code your senior engineer wrote last week is. It isn't stolen, but it also isn't copyrightable to your employer in any way that gives them the usual enforcement options. They can't sue someone who copies it. They can't claim it as a defendable asset. They can publish it, ship it, modify it, but the legal moat around it is thinner than they think.

sentences.en: The practical fallout: code that comes out of your AI tool more or less untouched may not be your company's property the way the code your senior engineer wrote last week is.
sentences.zh: 实际后果是：从你的人工智能工具中或多或少未受影响的代码可能不像你的高级工程师上周编写的代码那样是你公司的财产。

sentences.en: It isn't stolen, but it also isn't copyrightable to your employer in any way that gives them the usual enforcement options.
sentences.zh: 它没有被盗，但也不能以任何方式为您的雇主提供版权保护，从而为他们提供通常的执行选项。

sentences.en: They can't sue someone who copies it.
sentences.zh: 他们不能起诉复制它的人。

sentences.en: They can't claim it as a defendable asset.
sentences.zh: 他们不能将其视为可防御的资产。

sentences.en: They can publish it, ship it, modify it, but the legal moat around it is thinner than they think.
sentences.zh: 他们可以发布它、发布它、修改它，但围绕它的法律护城河比他们想象的要薄。

blocks.html_en: The picture changes once you cross a border. The United Kingdom has had Section 9(3) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 on the books since the late 1980s, and it explicitly addresses "computer-generated works," assigning authorship to "the person by whom the arrangements necessary for the creation of the work are undertaken." India follows a similar approach. The EU is in the middle of a more ambitious reframing through the AI Act, which we'll come back to. The point: there is no single global answer right now. The code your team writes from a London office may have a clearer ownership story than the same code written from San Francisco, on the same model, in the same week.

sentences.en: The picture changes once you cross a border.
sentences.zh: 一旦越过边界，情况就会发生变化。

sentences.en: The United Kingdom has had Section 9(3) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 on the books since the late 1980s, and it explicitly addresses "computer-generated works," assigning authorship to "the person by whom the arrangements necessary for the creation of the work are undertaken."
sentences.zh: 自 20 世纪 80 年代末以来，英国就已将《1988 年版权、设计和专利法》第 9(3) 条写入法律，其中明确涉及“计算机生成的作品”，将作者身份分配给“为创作作品进行必要安排的人”。

sentences.en: India follows a similar approach.
sentences.zh: 印度也采取了类似的做法。

sentences.en: The EU is in the middle of a more ambitious reframing through the AI Act, which we'll come back to.
sentences.zh: 欧盟正在通过《人工智能法案》进行更雄心勃勃的重组，我们稍后会讨论这一点。

sentences.en: The point: there is no single global answer right now.
sentences.zh: 要点是：目前还没有单一的全球答案。

sentences.en: The code your team writes from a London office may have a clearer ownership story than the same code written from San Francisco, on the same model, in the same week.
sentences.zh: 您的团队在伦敦办公室编写的代码可能比在旧金山、同一模型、同一周编写的相同代码具有更清晰的所有权故事。

blocks.html_en: What Your Tool's Contract Actually Says

sentences.en: What Your Tool's Contract Actually Says
sentences.zh: 你的工具的合同实际上说了些什么

blocks.html_en: Here is the sentence in GitHub's Copilot Product Specific Terms that does most of the work, and the sentence nobody quotes at AI adoption meetings:

sentences.en: Here is the sentence in GitHub's Copilot Product Specific Terms that does most of the work, and the sentence nobody quotes at AI adoption meetings:
sentences.zh: 以下是 GitHub 的 Copilot 产品特定条款中的句子，它完成了大部分工作，并且没有人在人工智能采用会议上引用这句话：

blocks.html_en: "You retain all responsibility for your Code, including Suggestions you include in your Code or reference to develop your Code."

sentences.en: "You retain all responsibility for your Code, including Suggestions you include in your Code or reference to develop your Code."
sentences.zh: “您保留对您的准则的所有责任，包括您在准则中包含的建议或制定准则的参考。”

blocks.html_en: "You retain all responsibility for your Code, including Suggestions you include in your Code or reference to develop your Code."

sentences.en: "You retain all responsibility for your Code, including Suggestions you include in your Code or reference to develop your Code."
sentences.zh: “您保留对您的准则的所有责任，包括您在准则中包含的建议或制定准则的参考。”

blocks.html_en: That sentence is doing two things at once. It hands you something: GitHub says it doesn't claim ownership of the suggestions, so they don't show up as a competing claim against your codebase. And it hands you something else: every legal risk that travels with those suggestions. Open-source license obligations from training data. Copyright claims from other developers whose code looked similar. Defects in the suggestion itself. The vendor's terms are explicit that you are responsible for determining whether your use of an output requires a third-party license and for complying with any such license.

sentences.en: That sentence is doing two things at once.
sentences.zh: 这句话同时做了两件事。

sentences.en: It hands you something: GitHub says it doesn't claim ownership of the suggestions, so they don't show up as a competing claim against your codebase.
sentences.zh: 它为你提供了一些东西：GitHub 表示它并不声称拥有这些建议的所有权，因此它们不会显示为针对你的代码库的竞争性主张。

sentences.en: And it hands you something else: every legal risk that travels with those suggestions.
sentences.zh: 它还为您提供了其他东西：伴随这些建议而来的所有法律风险。

sentences.en: Open-source license obligations from training data.
sentences.zh: 培训数据的开源许可义务。

sentences.en: Copyright claims from other developers whose code looked similar.
sentences.zh: 其他代码看起来相似的开发人员的版权声明。

sentences.en: Defects in the suggestion itself.
sentences.zh: 建议本身的缺陷。

sentences.en: The vendor's terms are explicit that you are responsible for determining whether your use of an output requires a third-party license and for complying with any such license.
sentences.zh: 供应商的条款明确规定，您有责任确定您对输出的使用是否需要第三方许可证并遵守任何此类许可证。

blocks.html_en: If you read GitHub's Customer Agreement the way developers read README files, skimming for the install instructions, you miss that line. If you read it the way a lawyer reads it, that line is the whole document.

sentences.en: If you read GitHub's Customer Agreement the way developers read README files, skimming for the install instructions, you miss that line.
sentences.zh: 如果您像开发人员阅读 README 文件一样阅读 GitHub 的客户协议，略读安装说明，您就会错过这一行。

sentences.en: If you read it the way a lawyer reads it, that line is the whole document.
sentences.zh: 如果您像律师那样阅读它，那么这一行就是整个文件。

blocks.html_en: This shape isn't unique to Copilot. The same pattern shows up in nearly every AI coding assistant's terms: vendor disclaims ownership of outputs, customer accepts responsibility for outputs. The reason it's the same pattern is that it's the only shape that lets the vendor operate. Imagine the alternative, where the vendor warrants that every suggestion is clean of license obligations, free of bugs, and immune to copyright challenge. They can't make that promise about a model trained on the entire public corpus of GitHub. So the contract pushes the risk to the only party who can plausibly evaluate it: the human merging the code.

sentences.en: This shape isn't unique to Copilot.
sentences.zh: 这种形状并不是 Copilot 所独有的。

sentences.en: The same pattern shows up in nearly every AI coding assistant's terms: vendor disclaims ownership of outputs, customer accepts responsibility for outputs.
sentences.zh: 几乎每个人工智能编码助理的术语中都出现了相同的模式：供应商否认输出的所有权，客户接受输出的责任。

sentences.en: The reason it's the same pattern is that it's the only shape that lets the vendor operate.
sentences.zh: 之所以采用相同的图案，是因为它是唯一允许供应商操作的形状。

sentences.en: Imagine the alternative, where the vendor warrants that every suggestion is clean of license obligations, free of bugs, and immune to copyright challenge.
sentences.zh: 想象一下另一种选择，供应商保证每项建议都没有许可义务，没有错误，并且不受版权挑战。

sentences.en: They can't make that promise about a model trained on the entire public corpus of GitHub.
sentences.zh: 他们无法对在 GitHub 的整个公共语料库上训练的模型做出这样的承诺。

sentences.en: So the contract pushes the risk to the only party who can plausibly evaluate it: the human merging the code.
sentences.zh: 因此，合约将风险推给了唯一能够合理评估风险的一方：合并代码的人。

blocks.html_en: The model can't read your codebase's license terms. The vendor doesn't know what regulator your industry answers to. The IDE doesn't know whether the project you're working on is a hobby site or a payments processor. The only place to anchor responsibility is where the merge button lives.

sentences.en: The model can't read your codebase's license terms.
sentences.zh: 该模型无法读取您的代码库的许可条款。

sentences.en: The vendor doesn't know what regulator your industry answers to.
sentences.zh: 供应商不知道您的行业对哪个监管机构负责。

sentences.en: The IDE doesn't know whether the project you're working on is a hobby site or a payments processor.
sentences.zh: IDE 不知道您正在处理的项目是业余爱好网站还是支付处理器。

sentences.zh: 唯一锚定责任的地方是合并按钮所在的地方。

blocks.html_en: The Indemnification Gap Nobody Mentions In Onboarding

sentences.en: The Indemnification Gap Nobody Mentions In Onboarding
sentences.zh: 入职时没有人提及的赔偿缺口

blocks.html_en: Here's where the contract gets uncomfortable, and where most engineering org charts have a blind spot.

sentences.en: Here's where the contract gets uncomfortable, and where most engineering org charts have a blind spot.
sentences.zh: 这就是合同让人感到不舒服的地方，也是大多数工程组织结构图存在盲点的地方。

blocks.html_en: GitHub does offer IP indemnification for suggestions, if you're on a Business or Enterprise plan. If you get sued for copyright infringement because Copilot reproduced a snippet of someone's code, Microsoft will step in to defend you and pay damages under its Customer Copyright Commitment. The same pattern holds across the industry: most major AI coding tools offer some form of indemnification at the enterprise tier.

sentences.en: GitHub does offer IP indemnification for suggestions, if you're on a Business or Enterprise plan.
sentences.zh: 如果您使用的是商业或企业计划，GitHub 确实为建议提供知识产权保护。

sentences.en: If you get sued for copyright infringement because Copilot reproduced a snippet of someone's code, Microsoft will step in to defend you and pay damages under its Customer Copyright Commitment.
sentences.zh: 如果您因为 Copilot 复制了某人的代码片段而被起诉侵犯版权，Microsoft 将介入为您辩护并根据其客户版权承诺支付损害赔偿金。

sentences.en: The same pattern holds across the industry: most major AI coding tools offer some form of indemnification at the enterprise tier.
sentences.zh: 整个行业都存在同样的模式：大多数主要的人工智能编码工具都在企业层面提供某种形式的赔偿。

blocks.html_en: If you're on the Individual plan, none of that applies. You get the same model, the same suggestions, the same risk surface, and zero legal cover. If a court eventually rules that a snippet Copilot suggested to you was a substantial reproduction of someone else's GPL-licensed code, you, personally, are the one with the problem.

sentences.en: If you're on the Individual plan, none of that applies.
sentences.zh: 如果您使用个人计划，则这些都不适用。

sentences.en: You get the same model, the same suggestions, the same risk surface, and zero legal cover.
sentences.zh: 您将获得相同的模型、相同的建议、相同的风险面和零法律保障。

sentences.en: If a court eventually rules that a snippet Copilot suggested to you was a substantial reproduction of someone else's GPL-licensed code, you, personally, are the one with the problem.
sentences.zh: 如果法院最终裁定 Copilot 向您建议的某个片段是对其他人 GPL 许可代码的实质性复制，那么您个人就是有问题的人。

blocks.html_en: The reason this matters beyond the lawyer's office: a lot of professional software still ships from teams where engineers use their personal Copilot subscriptions, either because the company hasn't standardized yet, or because someone signed up six months before the company adopted an enterprise tier, or because they like their personal account's settings better. The code those engineers produce flows into the company codebase, and the indemnification that the company assumes it has, because the company is paying for Enterprise, doesn't actually cover suggestions that were made through the personal subscription.

sentences.en: The reason this matters beyond the lawyer's office: a lot of professional software still ships from teams where engineers use their personal Copilot subscriptions, either because the company hasn't standardized yet, or because someone signed up six months before the company adopted an enterprise tier, or because they like their personal account's settings better.
sentences.zh: 这在律师办公室之外很重要的原因是：许多专业软件仍然来自工程师使用其个人 Copilot 订阅的团队，要么是因为该公司尚未标准化，要么是因为有人在公司采用企业级之前六个月注册了，或者是因为他们更喜欢自己的个人帐户设置。

sentences.en: The code those engineers produce flows into the company codebase, and the indemnification that the company assumes it has, because the company is paying for Enterprise, doesn't actually cover suggestions that were made through the personal subscription.
sentences.zh: 这些工程师生成的代码流入公司代码库，并且公司假设拥有的赔偿（因为公司为 Enterprise 付费）实际上并不涵盖通过个人订阅提出的建议。

blocks.html_en: Engineering leaders almost never audit this. The procurement team thinks the Enterprise contract covers everything. The developers don't know the contract draws a line at which subscription emitted the suggestion. The CISO finds out only if a lawsuit lands.

sentences.en: Engineering leaders almost never audit this.
sentences.zh: 工程领导者几乎从不对此进行审计。

sentences.en: The procurement team thinks the Enterprise contract covers everything.
sentences.zh: 采购团队认为企业合同涵盖了一切。

sentences.en: The developers don't know the contract draws a line at which subscription emitted the suggestion.
sentences.zh: 开发人员不知道合约在订阅发出建议时划定了界限。

sentences.en: The CISO finds out only if a lawsuit lands.
sentences.zh: CISO 只有在提起诉讼时才会知道。

blocks.html_en: A clean accountability story requires every IDE, every developer machine, and every shared environment to be running the indemnified tier. "We bought Enterprise" is not the same as "every suggestion that touches our repo came from Enterprise."

sentences.en: A clean accountability story requires every IDE, every developer machine, and every shared environment to be running the indemnified tier.
sentences.zh: 一个干净的责任故事要求每个 IDE、每个开发人员计算机和每个共享环境都运行受保障层。

sentences.en: "We bought Enterprise" is not the same as "every suggestion that touches our repo came from Enterprise."
sentences.zh: “我们购买了 Enterprise”与“涉及我们仓库的所有建议都来自 Enterprise”不同。

blocks.html_en: The 65-Lexeme Filter And What It Doesn't Catch

sentences.en: The 65-Lexeme Filter And What It Doesn't Catch
sentences.zh: 65-Lexeme 过滤器及其未捕获的内容

blocks.html_en: If you read GitHub's safety documentation, you'll find a feature called the duplicate detection filter. The filter checks suggestions against public code on GitHub, and if a suggestion contains a code segment of about 65 lexemes or more (roughly 150 characters, the length of one or two paragraphs of dense code) and matches public code closely enough, the suggestion is suppressed. Administrators can enable this filter at the enterprise level, and most reasonable adoption guidelines tell you to keep it on.

sentences.en: If you read GitHub's safety documentation, you'll find a feature called the duplicate detection filter.
sentences.zh: 如果您阅读 GitHub 的安全文档，您会发现一个称为重复检测过滤器的功能。

sentences.en: The filter checks suggestions against public code on GitHub, and if a suggestion contains a code segment of about 65 lexemes or more (roughly 150 characters, the length of one or two paragraphs of dense code) and matches public code closely enough, the suggestion is suppressed.
sentences.zh: 该过滤器会根据 GitHub 上的公共代码检查建议，如果建议包含大约 65 个词位或更多的代码段（大约 150 个字符，一两段密集代码的长度）并且与公共代码足够接近，则该建议会被抑制。

sentences.en: Administrators can enable this filter at the enterprise level, and most reasonable adoption guidelines tell you to keep it on.
sentences.zh: 管理员可以在企业级别启用此过滤器，并且最合理的采用指南会告诉您保持打开状态。

blocks.html_en: The filter is the single most-cited mitigation when teams talk about Copilot license risk. It's also a great example of the gap between "a feature exists" and "the feature solves the problem."

sentences.en: The filter is the single most-cited mitigation when teams talk about Copilot license risk.
sentences.zh: 当团队谈论 Copilot 许可证风险时，过滤器是最常被引用的缓解措施。

sentences.en: It's also a great example of the gap between "a feature exists" and "the feature solves the problem."
sentences.zh: 这也是“功能存在”和“功能解决问题”之间差距的一个很好的例子。

blocks.html_en: The 65-lexeme threshold isn't arbitrary. It's a tradeoff between recall and usefulness. A filter that suppressed any three-token match against public code would suppress almost every Copilot suggestion, because three-token sequences appear in nearly all open-source projects. So the threshold sits well above that, in a band where matches are likely to be intentionally similar rather than accidentally similar. Reasonable design. The cost is that anything under that threshold flows through unfiltered.

sentences.en: The 65-lexeme threshold isn't arbitrary.
sentences.zh: 65 个词位阈值不是任意的。

sentences.en: It's a tradeoff between recall and usefulness.
sentences.zh: 这是召回率和实用性之间的权衡。

sentences.en: A filter that suppressed any three-token match against public code would suppress almost every Copilot suggestion, because three-token sequences appear in nearly all open-source projects.
sentences.zh: 抑制与公共代码的任何三令牌匹配的过滤器将抑制几乎所有 Copilot 建议，因为三令牌序列出现在几乎所有开源项目中。

sentences.en: So the threshold sits well above that, in a band where matches are likely to be intentionally similar rather than accidentally similar.
sentences.zh: 因此，在匹配可能是有意相似而不是偶然相似的范围内，阈值远高于该阈值。

sentences.en: Reasonable design.
sentences.zh: 设计合理。

sentences.en: The cost is that anything under that threshold flows through unfiltered.
sentences.zh: 代价是任何低于该阈值的东西都未经过滤地流过。

blocks.html_en: A 30-lexeme function body that exactly matches a snippet from a GPL-licensed repository can land in your codebase, and the filter never sees it. A 50-lexeme idiom, a common algorithm implementation, a parser fragment, a serialization helper, can land identical to dozens of repositories without ever crossing the line. The filter isn't claiming to catch everything. It's claiming to catch the long-form near-duplicates that are most likely to support a copyright claim. The short-form stuff lives in a grey zone where the law itself hasn't decided what "substantially similar" means.

sentences.en: A 30-lexeme function body that exactly matches a snippet from a GPL-licensed repository can land in your codebase, and the filter never sees it.
sentences.zh: 与 GPL 许可的存储库中的代码片段完全匹配的 30 个词位函数体可以进入您的代码库，而过滤器永远不会看到它。

sentences.en: A 50-lexeme idiom, a common algorithm implementation, a parser fragment, a serialization helper, can land identical to dozens of repositories without ever crossing the line.
sentences.zh: 一个 50 个词位的习惯用法、一个通用的算法实现、一个解析器片段、一个序列化助手，可以在数十个存储库中获得相同的结果，而无需越界。

sentences.en: The filter isn't claiming to catch everything.
sentences.zh: 该过滤器并不声称可以捕获所有内容。

sentences.en: It's claiming to catch the long-form near-duplicates that are most likely to support a copyright claim.
sentences.zh: 它声称能够捕获最有可能支持版权声明的长篇近似重复内容。

sentences.en: The short-form stuff lives in a grey zone where the law itself hasn't decided what "substantially similar" means.
sentences.zh: 简短的内容处于一个灰色地带，法律本身尚未确定“实质上相似”的含义。

blocks.html_en: This is the part where you make a defensible call instead of a perfect one. The filter helps. Treating it as a complete solution doesn't. Treat it like the spam filter in your inbox: useful, often quietly correct, but not a substitute for not opening attachments from strangers.

sentences.en: This is the part where you make a defensible call instead of a perfect one.
sentences.zh: 在这一部分，你会做出有道理的决定，而不是完美的决定。

sentences.en: The filter helps.
sentences.zh: 过滤器有帮助。

sentences.en: Treating it as a complete solution doesn't.
sentences.zh: 将其视为完整的解决方案则不然。

sentences.en: Treat it like the spam filter in your inbox: useful, often quietly correct, but not a substitute for not opening attachments from strangers.
sentences.zh: 将其视为收件箱中的垃圾邮件过滤器：有用，通常会悄悄正确，但不能替代不打开陌生人的附件。

blocks.html_en: The Regulators Don't Care Who Wrote It

sentences.en: The Regulators Don't Care Who Wrote It
sentences.zh: 监管机构不在乎谁写的

blocks.html_en: The U.S. copyright conversation focuses on ownership. The European regulatory conversation focuses on responsibility for outcomes, and the framing is genuinely different.

sentences.en: The U.S. copyright conversation focuses on ownership.
sentences.zh: 美国版权对话的重点是所有权。

sentences.en: The European regulatory conversation focuses on responsibility for outcomes, and the framing is genuinely different.
sentences.zh: 欧洲监管对话的重点是对结果的责任，框架确实不同。

blocks.html_en: Three pieces of EU legislation are converging into what some practitioners are calling the "Compliance Trinity": the AI Act, the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA), and the Product Liability Directive (PLD). Each one approaches AI from a different angle. The AI Act regulates AI systems by risk class, the CRA imposes cybersecurity obligations on connected products, the PLD attaches liability for defective products including software. They all share a common assumption: regulators do not care whether your code was written by a human, an AI tool, a contractor, or a small team of trained ferrets. They care about the product that reaches the market.

sentences.en: Three pieces of EU legislation are converging into what some practitioners are calling the "Compliance Trinity": the AI Act, the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA), and the Product Liability Directive (PLD).
sentences.zh: 欧盟的三项立法正在融合成一些从业者所说的“合规三位一体”：人工智能法案、网络弹性法案（CRA）和产品责任指令（PLD）。

sentences.en: Each one approaches AI from a different angle.
sentences.zh: 每个人都从不同的角度看待人工智能。

sentences.en: The AI Act regulates AI systems by risk class, the CRA imposes cybersecurity obligations on connected products, the PLD attaches liability for defective products including software.
sentences.zh: 《人工智能法案》按风险类别对人工智能系统进行监管，CRA 对互联产品规定了网络安全义务，PLD 对包括软件在内的缺陷产品规定了责任。

sentences.en: They all share a common assumption: regulators do not care whether your code was written by a human, an AI tool, a contractor, or a small team of trained ferrets.
sentences.zh: 他们都有一个共同的假设：监管机构并不关心你的代码是由人类、人工智能工具、承包商还是一小群训练有素的雪貂编写的。

sentences.en: They care about the product that reaches the market.
sentences.zh: 他们关心进入市场的产品。

blocks.html_en: August 2, 2026 is the milestone most developers will hear about. It's when many of the AI Act's substantive obligations kick in, including transparency requirements for AI-generated content and active enforcement powers. Plain developer-assistance AI use generally doesn't drag a system into the high-risk category by itself, but AI used for things like worker evaluation or critical-component generation absolutely can. The classification depends on what the system does in the world, not on what tool helped write it.

sentences.en: August 2, 2026 is the milestone most developers will hear about.
sentences.zh: 2026 年 8 月 2 日是大多数开发者都会听说的里程碑。

sentences.en: It's when many of the AI Act's substantive obligations kick in, including transparency requirements for AI-generated content and active enforcement powers.
sentences.zh: 《人工智能法案》的许多实质性义务此时开始生效，包括对人工智能生成内容的透明度要求和积极的执行权力。

sentences.en: Plain developer-assistance AI use generally doesn't drag a system into the high-risk category by itself, but AI used for things like worker evaluation or critical-component generation absolutely can.
sentences.zh: 普通的开发人员协助人工智能的使用本身通常不会将系统拖入高风险类别，但用于工人评估或关键组件生成等事情的人工智能绝对可以。

sentences.en: The classification depends on what the system does in the world, not on what tool helped write it.
sentences.zh: 分类取决于系统在世界上的用途，而不是帮助编写它的工具。

blocks.html_en: The harder shift is the cultural one this implies. Before AI assistants, the was-it-written-carefully question and the is-the-product-safe question pointed at roughly the same thing: the people who wrote the code were also the people responsible for it. With AI assistants involved, the two questions can drift apart. The product can be unsafe even if everyone who reviewed the diff would swear they read it carefully. The regulator doesn't accept "the AI suggested it" as an explanation any more than they would accept "the contractor wrote it." You shipped it; you're responsible for it.

sentences.en: The harder shift is the cultural one this implies.
sentences.zh: 更困难的转变是这意味着文化上的转变。

sentences.en: Before AI assistants, the was-it-written-carefully question and the is-the-product-safe question pointed at roughly the same thing: the people who wrote the code were also the people responsible for it.
sentences.zh: 在人工智能助手出现之前，“编写是否仔细”的问题和“产品是否安全”的问题大致指向同一件事：编写代码的人也是对其负责的人。

sentences.en: With AI assistants involved, the two questions can drift apart.
sentences.zh: 当人工智能助手介入时，这两个问题可能会出现分歧。

sentences.en: The product can be unsafe even if everyone who reviewed the diff would swear they read it carefully.
sentences.zh: 即使每个查看差异的人都发誓他们仔细阅读了该差异，该产品也可能不安全。

sentences.en: The regulator doesn't accept "the AI suggested it" as an explanation any more than they would accept "the contractor wrote it."
sentences.zh: 监管机构不接受“人工智能建议”作为解释，就像他们不接受“承包商写的”一样。

sentences.en: You shipped it; you're responsible for it.
sentences.zh: 你发货了；你要对此负责。

blocks.html_en: That's a clean framing, once you accept it. The discomfort comes from a half-formed expectation that AI tools come with a kind of distributed responsibility, that the vendor shares the blame because they trained the model. They don't. They explicitly don't, contractually. And the regulators have politely declined to invent that kind of shared responsibility either.

sentences.en: That's a clean framing, once you accept it.
sentences.zh: 一旦你接受了，这就是一个干净的框架。

sentences.en: The discomfort comes from a half-formed expectation that AI tools come with a kind of distributed responsibility, that the vendor shares the blame because they trained the model.
sentences.zh: 这种不适来自于一种不成熟的期望，即人工智能工具具有某种分布式责任，供应商也分担责任，因为他们训练了模型。

sentences.en: They don't.
sentences.zh: 他们不这样做。

sentences.en: They explicitly don't, contractually.
sentences.zh: 根据合同，他们明确不这样做。

sentences.en: And the regulators have politely declined to invent that kind of shared responsibility either.
sentences.zh: 监管机构也礼貌地拒绝发明这种共同责任。

blocks.html_en: The Cultural Problem Nobody Warns You About

sentences.en: The Cultural Problem Nobody Warns You About
sentences.zh: 没有人警告你的文化问题

blocks.html_en: Outside the legal text, there's a softer pattern that quietly erodes accountability inside engineering teams, and it has nothing to do with contracts.

sentences.en: Outside the legal text, there's a softer pattern that quietly erodes accountability inside engineering teams, and it has nothing to do with contracts.
sentences.zh: 在法律文本之外，还有一种更温和的模式悄悄地侵蚀了工程团队内部的责任，而且它与合同无关。

blocks.html_en: Researchers studying AI-assisted development have started to document something most senior engineers already feel: code that comes out of an AI assistant tends to get less careful review than code a human typed. Reviewers spend less time on it. They flag fewer issues. They're more likely to wave through a function that compiles and looks reasonable. Part of this is fatigue, because there's more code to review now than ever, now that the typing constraint that used to throttle output is gone. Part of it is implicit trust: the model has read more code than any reviewer alive, so its output gets a small unconscious authority bump. Part of it is just the design of the interface, which makes acceptance the default and rejection the friction.

sentences.en: Researchers studying AI-assisted development have started to document something most senior engineers already feel: code that comes out of an AI assistant tends to get less careful review than code a human typed.
sentences.zh: 研究人工智能辅助开发的研究人员已经开始记录大多数高级工程师已经感受到的东西：人工智能助手生成的代码往往比人类输入的代码得到的审查不那么仔细。

sentences.en: Reviewers spend less time on it.
sentences.zh: 审稿人花在上面的时间更少。

sentences.en: They flag fewer issues.
sentences.zh: 他们标记的问题较少。

sentences.en: They're more likely to wave through a function that compiles and looks reasonable.
sentences.zh: 他们更有可能通过一个可以编译且看起来合理的函数。

sentences.en: Part of this is fatigue, because there's more code to review now than ever, now that the typing constraint that used to throttle output is gone.
sentences.zh: 部分原因是疲劳，因为现在需要检查的代码比以往任何时候都多，而用于限制输出的类型约束已经消失。

sentences.en: Part of it is implicit trust: the model has read more code than any reviewer alive, so its output gets a small unconscious authority bump.
sentences.zh: 其中一部分是隐性信任：该模型阅读的代码比任何活着的审阅者都多，因此它的输出会无意识地获得一个小的权威提升。

sentences.en: Part of it is just the design of the interface, which makes acceptance the default and rejection the friction.
sentences.zh: 部分原因只是界面的设计，这使得接受成为默认，拒绝成为摩擦。

blocks.html_en: The problem isn't that any of those reactions is unreasonable on its own. It's that they compound in the direction of less accountability, not more. The diff is twice as long, the review is half as careful, and the person who would normally have asked a sharp question about a defensive null check is busy scrolling through the next three suggestions. The unit of human attention per line of merged code drops, and so does the chance of catching the bug, the license issue, or the subtle authorization mistake that the model didn't realize it was making.

sentences.en: The problem isn't that any of those reactions is unreasonable on its own.
sentences.zh: 问题不在于这些反应本身是不合理的。

sentences.en: It's that they compound in the direction of less accountability, not more.
sentences.zh: 它们的复合方向是减少责任，而不是增加责任。

sentences.en: The diff is twice as long, the review is half as careful, and the person who would normally have asked a sharp question about a defensive null check is busy scrolling through the next three suggestions.
sentences.zh: diff 的长度是原来的两倍，审查的仔细程度是原来的一半，通常会提出有关防御性空检查的尖锐问题的人正忙着滚动浏览接下来的三个建议。

sentences.en: The unit of human attention per line of merged code drops, and so does the chance of catching the bug, the license issue, or the subtle authorization mistake that the model didn't realize it was making.
sentences.zh: 每行合并代码的人类注意力单位下降，捕获错误、许可证问题或模型没有意识到自己正在犯的微妙授权错误的机会也随之下降。

blocks.html_en: This isn't an argument against AI tools. It's the missing line on the dashboard that most teams haven't drawn yet: if we're producing more code, are we proportionally reviewing more carefully, or are we relaxing review because the volume forced us to? Nobody asks that question out loud. It would slow things down. And nothing about the regulatory framework above will save you from the answer.

sentences.en: This isn't an argument against AI tools.
sentences.zh: 这并不是反对人工智能工具。

sentences.en: It's the missing line on the dashboard that most teams haven't drawn yet: if we're producing more code, are we proportionally reviewing more carefully, or are we relaxing review because the volume forced us to?
sentences.zh: 这是大多数团队尚未绘制的仪表板上缺失的一行：如果我们生成更多代码，我们是按比例更仔细地审查，还是因为数量迫使我们放松审查？

sentences.en: Nobody asks that question out loud.
sentences.zh: 没有人大声问这个问题。

sentences.en: It would slow things down.
sentences.zh: 它会减慢速度。

sentences.en: And nothing about the regulatory framework above will save you from the answer.
sentences.zh: 上述监管框架并不能帮助您找到答案。

blocks.html_en: What A Working Accountability Model Looks Like

sentences.en: What A Working Accountability Model Looks Like
sentences.zh: 工作责任模型是什么样的

blocks.html_en: You can't solve any of this with a Slack post. You can't solve it with a policy document either, unless the policy actually changes how code lands in your repo. The teams that handle this well do four mundane things, none of them clever.

sentences.en: You can't solve any of this with a Slack post.

content_html_zh:
想象一下您的 AI 助手刚刚生成了 200 行代码。 从法律上讲，你可能不拥有它的一行，但从法律上讲，你仍然要为它带来的每个错误负责。 这是这两句话之间的差距，以及它对您明天合并的代码意味着什么。

这就是整个主题存在的空白。 生成代码的工具不能被起诉。 模型不能承担责任。 供应商的合同已经用大多数开发人员从未读过的语言明确表示，责任会流向按 Tab 的人。

有趣的部分并不在于这种差距的存在。 工程界几乎没有人像现在这样对待它。 我们花了两年的时间来庆祝生产力，并用了一小部分时间来弄清楚当速度满足版权声明、合规性审计或生产事件时该怎么做。 让我们来分解一下：法院实际上说了些什么，你的工具的合同实际上说了些什么，法律悬崖在哪里，以及一个理智的工程团队如何处理整个事情，而不禁止人工智能或假装问题不存在。

无人签署的交接

软件所有权曾经是一个已解决的问题。 你编写了代码，你的雇主付钱给你编写代码，你的雇佣合同将版权分配给他们，公司根据他们喜欢的任何许可证发布它。 三方（您、您的雇主、用户）以及他们之间的界限是清晰的。 如果出现问题，用户会向公司投诉，公司会查看提交历史记录，然后有人进行一对一的安静对话。

人工智能助手将第四方滑入该图片中，该方的合同看起来与其他方都不一样。 该模特没有签署你的雇佣协议。 供应商不参与您的发布过程。 他们的 EULA 并不像雇佣合同；而是像雇佣合同一样。它的形状像免责声明。 你得到建议，保留建议，还保留其附带的所有内容：错误、许可义务、安全漏洞，以及未来监管机构决定检查建议如何到达那里的任何内容。

这种交接悄然发生。 您的 IDE 中没有复选框显示“我对此完成承担法律责任”。 您只需按 Tab 键即可。 该界面的设计感觉就像自动完成，大多数开发人员将自动完成的心理模型扩展到它：编辑器正在帮助我更快地打字，代码仍然是我的，我对代码库的所有权没有改变。 这种心理模型几乎是正确的，也几乎是完全错误的，这取决于你问哪个问题。

法院实际判决了什么

目前美国法律形势的核心有两件事，当开发商听到这两件事时，他们都感到惊讶。

第一：在美国，版权保护需要人类作者。 这不是一个新规则。 早在 2018 年，这条规则就决定猴子不能对自拍照提出版权主张，但它在泰勒诉美国案中接受了最新的压力测试。 Perlmutter，一个关于人工智能系统在没有有意义的人类输入的情况下生成图像的案例。 2025 年 3 月，华盛顿特区 巡回法院维持了下级法院的裁决：1976 年《版权法》“要求所有符合资格的作品首先必须由人类创作”。 没有人类作者，没有版权。 输出位于公共领域。

第二个是开发者实际居住的地方：美国版权局在 2024 年和 2025 年初试图为确实涉及人类的人工智能辅助工作划定界限。 2025 年 1 月，该办公室发布了指导意见，称只有当人类贡献“足够的表达元素”时，人工智能输出才有资格获得版权。 单独的提示，即使是复杂的、迭代的提示，也是不够的。 该办公室报告中引用的一条评论提出了一个比开发人员预期更严重的比喻：重复提示就像旋转轮盘赌。 人类选择了，但人类没有足够明确地控制输出的表达元素，无法被视为作者。

这个比喻比它周围的措辞更重要。 从版权的角度来说，“Tab 接受”就是旋转轮子。 “Tab 接受，然后重写四行，重构函数，添加一个保护子句，并将其集成到您已经设计的类中”是另一回事。 这就是人类贡献开始成为办公室所寻求的表达控制的地方。

实际后果是：从你的人工智能工具中或多或少未受影响的代码可能不像你的高级工程师上周编写的代码那样是你公司的财产。 它没有被盗，但也不能以任何方式为您的雇主提供版权保护，从而为他们提供通常的执行选项。 他们不能起诉复制它的人。 他们不能将其视为可防御的资产。 他们可以发布它、发布它、修改它，但围绕它的法律护城河比他们想象的要薄。

一旦越过边界，情况就会发生变化。 自 20 世纪 80 年代末以来，英国就已将《1988 年版权、设计和专利法》第 9(3) 条写入法律，其中明确涉及“计算机生成的作品”，将作者身份分配给“为创作作品进行必要安排的人”。 印度也采取了类似的做法。 欧盟正在通过《人工智能法案》进行更雄心勃勃的重组，我们稍后会讨论这一点。 要点是：目前还没有单一的全球答案。 您的团队在伦敦办公室编写的代码可能比在旧金山、同一模型、同一周编写的相同代码具有更清晰的所有权故事。

你的工具的合同实际上说了些什么

以下是 GitHub 的 Copilot 产品特定条款中的句子，它完成了大部分工作，并且没有人在人工智能采用会议上引用这句话：

“您保留对您的准则的所有责任，包括您在准则中包含的建议或制定准则的参考。”

这句话同时做了两件事。 它为你提供了一些东西：GitHub 表示它并不声称拥有这些建议的所有权，因此它们不会显示为针对你的代码库的竞争性主张。 它还为您提供了其他东西：伴随这些建议而来的所有法律风险。 培训数据的开源许可义务。 其他代码看起来相似的开发人员的版权声明。 建议本身的缺陷。 供应商的条款明确规定，您有责任确定您对输出的使用是否需要第三方许可证并遵守任何此类许可证。

如果您像开发人员阅读 README 文件一样阅读 GitHub 的客户协议，略读安装说明，您就会错过这一行。 如果您像律师那样阅读它，那么这一行就是整个文件。

这种形状并不是 Copilot 所独有的。 几乎每个人工智能编码助理的术语中都出现了相同的模式：供应商否认输出的所有权，客户接受输出的责任。 之所以采用相同的图案，是因为它是唯一允许供应商操作的形状。 想象一下另一种选择，供应商保证每项建议都没有许可义务，没有错误，并且不受版权挑战。 他们无法对在 GitHub 的整个公共语料库上训练的模型做出这样的承诺。 因此，合约将风险推给了唯一能够合理评估风险的一方：合并代码的人。

该模型无法读取您的代码库的许可条款。 供应商不知道您的行业对哪个监管机构负责。 IDE 不知道您正在处理的项目是业余爱好网站还是支付处理器。 唯一锚定责任的地方是合并按钮所在的地方。

入职时没有人提及的赔偿缺口

这就是合同让人感到不舒服的地方，也是大多数工程组织结构图存在盲点的地方。

如果您使用的是商业或企业计划，GitHub 确实为建议提供知识产权保护。 如果您因为 Copilot 复制了某人的代码片段而被起诉侵犯版权，Microsoft 将介入为您辩护并根据其客户版权承诺支付损害赔偿金。 整个行业都存在同样的模式：大多数主要的人工智能编码工具都在企业层面提供某种形式的赔偿。

如果您使用个人计划，则这些都不适用。 您将获得相同的模型、相同的建议、相同的风险面和零法律保障。 如果法院最终裁定 Copilot 向您建议的某个片段是对其他人 GPL 许可代码的实质性复制，那么您个人就是有问题的人。

这在律师办公室之外很重要的原因是：许多专业软件仍然来自工程师使用其个人 Copilot 订阅的团队，要么是因为该公司尚未标准化，要么是因为有人在公司采用企业级之前六个月注册了，或者是因为他们更喜欢自己的个人帐户设置。 这些工程师生成的代码流入公司代码库，并且公司假设拥有的赔偿（因为公司为 Enterprise 付费）实际上并不涵盖通过个人订阅提出的建议。

工程领导者几乎从不对此进行审计。 采购团队认为企业合同涵盖了一切。 开发人员不知道合约在订阅发出建议时划定了界限。 CISO 只有在提起诉讼时才会知道。

一个干净的责任故事要求每个 IDE、每个开发人员计算机和每个共享环境都运行受保障层。 “我们购买了 Enterprise”与“涉及我们仓库的所有建议都来自 Enterprise”不同。

65-Lexeme 过滤器及其未捕获的内容

如果您阅读 GitHub 的安全文档，您会发现一个称为重复检测过滤器的功能。 该过滤器会根据 GitHub 上的公共代码检查建议，如果建议包含大约 65 个词位或更多的代码段（大约 150 个字符，一两段密集代码的长度）并且与公共代码足够接近，则该建议会被抑制。 管理员可以在企业级别启用此过滤器，并且最合理的采用指南会告诉您保持打开状态。

当团队谈论 Copilot 许可证风险时，过滤器是最常被引用的缓解措施。 这也是“功能存在”和“功能解决问题”之间差距的一个很好的例子。

65 个词位阈值不是任意的。 这是召回率和实用性之间的权衡。 抑制与公共代码的任何三令牌匹配的过滤器将抑制几乎所有 Copilot 建议，因为三令牌序列出现在几乎所有开源项目中。 因此，在匹配可能是有意相似而不是偶然相似的范围内，阈值远高于该阈值。 设计合理。 代价是任何低于该阈值的东西都未经过滤地流过。

与 GPL 许可的存储库中的代码片段完全匹配的 30 个词位函数体可以进入您的代码库，而过滤器永远不会看到它。 一个 50 个词位的习惯用法、一个通用的算法实现、一个解析器片段、一个序列化助手，可以在数十个存储库中获得相同的结果，而无需越界。 该过滤器并不声称可以捕获所有内容。 它声称能够捕获最有可能支持版权声明的长篇近似重复内容。 简短的内容处于一个灰色地带，法律本身尚未确定“实质上相似”的含义。

在这一部分，你会做出有道理的决定，而不是完美的决定。 过滤器有帮助。 将其视为完整的解决方案则不然。 将其视为收件箱中的垃圾邮件过滤器：有用，通常会悄悄正确，但不能替代不打开陌生人的附件。

监管机构不在乎谁写的

美国版权对话的重点是所有权。 欧洲监管对话的重点是对结果的责任，框架确实不同。

欧盟的三项立法正在融合成一些从业者所说的“合规三位一体”：人工智能法案、网络弹性法案（CRA）和产品责任指令（PLD）。 每个人都从不同的角度看待人工智能。 《人工智能法案》按风险类别对人工智能系统进行监管，CRA 对互联产品规定了网络安全义务，PLD 对包括软件在内的缺陷产品规定了责任。 他们都有一个共同的假设：监管机构并不关心你的代码是由人类、人工智能工具、承包商还是一小群训练有素的雪貂编写的。 他们关心进入市场的产品。

2026 年 8 月 2 日是大多数开发者都会听说的里程碑。 《人工智能法案》的许多实质性义务此时开始生效，包括对人工智能生成内容的透明度要求和积极的执行权力。 普通的开发人员协助人工智能的使用本身通常不会将系统拖入高风险类别，但用于工人评估或关键组件生成等事情的人工智能绝对可以。 分类取决于系统在世界上的用途，而不是帮助编写它的工具。

更困难的转变是这意味着文化上的转变。 在人工智能助手出现之前，“编写是否仔细”的问题和“产品是否安全”的问题大致指向同一件事：编写代码的人也是对其负责的人。 当人工智能助手介入时，这两个问题可能会出现分歧。 即使每个查看差异的人都发誓他们仔细阅读了该差异，该产品也可能不安全。 监管机构不接受“人工智能建议”作为解释，就像他们不接受“承包商写的”一样。 你发货了；你要对此负责。

一旦你接受了，这就是一个干净的框架。 这种不适来自于一种不成熟的期望，即人工智能工具具有某种分布式责任，供应商也分担责任，因为他们训练了模型。 他们不这样做。 根据合同，他们明确不这样做。 监管机构也礼貌地拒绝发明这种共同责任。

没有人警告你的文化问题

在法律文本之外，还有一种更温和的模式悄悄地侵蚀了工程团队内部的责任，而且它与合同无关。

研究人工智能辅助开发的研究人员已经开始记录大多数高级工程师已经感受到的东西：人工智能助手生成的代码往往比人类输入的代码得到的审查不那么仔细。 审稿人花在上面的时间更少。 他们标记的问题较少。 他们更有可能通过一个可以编译且看起来合理的函数。 部分原因是疲劳，因为现在需要检查的代码比以往任何时候都多，而用于限制输出的类型约束已经消失。 其中一部分是隐性信任：该模型阅读的代码比任何活着的审阅者都多，因此它的输出会无意识地获得一个小的权威提升。 部分原因只是界面的设计，这使得接受成为默认，拒绝成为摩擦。

问题不在于这些反应本身是不合理的。 它们的复合方向是减少责任，而不是增加责任。 diff 的长度是原来的两倍，审查的仔细程度是原来的一半，通常会提出有关防御性空检查的尖锐问题的人正忙着滚动浏览接下来的三个建议。 每行合并代码的人类注意力单位下降，捕获错误、许可证问题或模型没有意识到自己正在犯的微妙授权错误的机会也随之下降。

这并不是反对人工智能工具。 这是大多数团队尚未绘制的仪表板上缺失的一行：如果我们生成更多代码，我们是按比例更仔细地审查，还是因为数量迫使我们放松审查？ 没有人大声问这个问题。 它会减慢速度。 上述监管框架并不能帮助您找到答案。

工作责任模型是什么样的

你无法通过 Slack 帖子解决任何问题。 您也无法通过策略文档来解决它，除非该策略实际上改变了代码在您的存储库中的存放方式。 处理得很好的团队做了四件平凡的事情，但都不聪明。

他们让层级所有权变得无聊。 每个使用 AI 助手的开发人员、每个共享环境、每个 CI 运行者都处于受保护层。 公司工作没有个人订阅逃生通道。 这是采购层面的无聊决定，通过一项动议缩小了大部分差距。

它们默认启用重复过滤并且可验证。 该过滤器在管理控制台中启用，它是定期安全审查的一部分，与 SSO 强制执行方式相同。 这不是一个有人可能会在周二改变的设置；这是经过审计的控制。

他们在代码审查中将人工智能生成的代码视为与第三方依赖项相同。 从心理上来说，模型中的新代码的处理方式就像高级工程师处理 2014 年 Stack Overflow 答案中的复制粘贴一样。 你问：我是否理解它的作用，我是否知道它来自的许可证形状，如果模型不在这里指向，我会在上面签名吗？ 这个问题是整个道德和法律机构，被压缩成审稿人实际上可以内化的句子。

他们指定了一个所有者。 每个 PR 都有一个人负责其中的代码。 不是型号。 不是供应商。 不是“团队”。 一个有名字的人，如果密码被破解，他会接电话。 如果你的工具让人工智能重度 PR 很难记住那个人是谁，那么这是一个值得解决的流程问题，因为上面的所有其他框架只有在其末尾有一个名字时才有效。

注意 所有这些都不需要法律团队或合规官员。 前三个步骤是管理控制台设置和审查清单句子。 第四个是优秀团队已经拥有的针对人类编写代码的文化规范。 人工智能引入的唯一新事物是你无法跳过其中任何一个，因为数量增加了。

概括整个事情的句子

如果你记得这篇文章中的一件事，那就这样吧：每一个人工智能建议都是来自匿名第三方的贡献，他阅读了互联网上的每个开源项目，你无法采访他，并且你的合同明确将他排除在责任链之外。

这并不是停止使用人工智能助手的理由。 这甚至不是放慢脚步的理由。 这是关系的实际形状，软件工程中的摩擦不断地回到它身上，因为这种形状不会自行消失。 法院正在对此达成一致。 供应商已经围绕它制定了合同。 监管机构即将强制执行。

好消息（如果你可以这样称呼的话）是答案从来不在模型中。 它属于工作流程中最无聊、最严格的部分，在这部分中，人们查看差异并决定是否合并。 这部分从未像现在这样重要，而您的团队在处理整个所有权问题时可以做的最便宜的事情就是确保该部分仍然发生，并像在建议开始每分钟两次到达之前一样小心。

您编写了合并提交。 你拥有它。
